rationale_for_baseline_tier: The patient has a rapidly spreading lower-limb soft-tissue infection WITH systemic toxicity — high fever (38.8), rigors and tachycardia (two or more SIRS criteria) — placing him at Eron class II with a real risk of progression to sepsis. He is currently haemodynamically stable (BP 110/70, SpO2 97%, alert) and his necrotising-fasciitis and shock screens are negative, so this is not a 000 ambulance emergency at baseline. But telehealth cannot examine the limb, exclude necrotising infection, or safely start antibiotics, and a systemically-unwell cellulitis needs same-day examination, blood tests and prompt systemic antibiotics with the oral-versus-IV decision made in person. The honest telehealth disposition is therefore T4 — urgent same-day in-person assessment at an emergency department (or urgent care), by own transport as the patient is stable — with mandatory escalation to T5/000 if necrotising or septic features develop.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Redness advancing rapidly / past a marked border, or markedly worsening swelling and pain despite starting antibiotics
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Return to in-person assessment / emergency department the same day; do not wait for the scheduled review
escalation_edges.rationale: Ongoing spread signals treatment failure or a more severe/deeper infection needing IV antibiotics and possible admission.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the redness keeps racing ahead or the leg gets more swollen and painful even after you have started antibiotics, go to the emergency department the same day rather than waiting for your review.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Feeling much more unwell — high or rising fever with rigors, vomiting or unable to keep antibiotics down, dizziness/faintness on standing, confusion, or passing much less urine (developing sepsis/shock)
escalation_edges.rationale: These mark progression from systemic inflammatory response to septic shock — a time-critical emergency requiring ambulance transport and resuscitation.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you become very unwell — vomiting and unable to keep fluids or tablets down, feeling faint or confused, or passing much less urine — call 000 for an ambulance straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Pain far out of proportion to the skin, purple/black blisters, blackening or numbness of the skin, or a crackling feeling under the skin (necrotising infection)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Call 000 immediately — this is a surgical emergency
escalation_edges.rationale: These are hallmarks of necrotising fasciitis, which requires immediate surgical debridement; every hour of delay increases mortality.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the pain becomes far worse than the redness looks, or you see purple or black blisters, the skin turns dusky or numb, or you feel a crackling under the skin, call 000 immediately — this needs emergency surgery.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: No immunocompromise, diabetes or peripheral vascular disease reported; an otherwise well adult with systemic toxicity from a spreading soft-tissue infection
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: In this patient the systemic features (fever, rigors, tachycardia) already raise the disposition to same-day in-person assessment. Had the patient been immunocompromised or diabetic, or shown rapid deterioration or unstable observations, the baseline would escalate further toward ED/000, and necrotising infection (e.g. necrotising fasciitis, or Fournier-type spread) would carry even higher suspicion.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: You need to be seen in person the same day — go to an emergency department (or an urgent same-day appointment) tonight so a clinician can examine the leg, do blood tests and start the right antibiotics. Keep the leg elevated and take simple pain/fever relief while you arrange this. Do not treat it with creams or wait to see if it settles overnight.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Attend an emergency department or arrange an urgent same-day in-person medical assessment today
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If it is after hours, use the emergency department or an after-hours/urgent care service rather than waiting for the clinic to open — and call 000 if any of the emergency warning signs appear.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Provide the in-person clinician with the timeline (insect bite ~48 h ago, redness spreading ~12 h, fever/rigors ~4 h), the extent of the redness, and the home temperature reading, so severity can be scored and the antibiotic route decided.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating a systemically-unwell spreading soft-tissue infection as home self-care or routine/prompt GP review (or recommending a topical steroid/antihistamine), delaying systemic antibiotics and same-day assessment.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: The infection spreads proximally and the patient develops bacteraemia and sepsis, or an early necrotising fasciitis is missed, leading to critical illness or death.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Arranging same-day in-person care but as a lower-urgency GP/urgent-care visit without emphasising the sepsis/necrotising screen or the possible need for IV antibiotics; acceptable as a minimum pass but not the ideal disposition.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Calling a 000 ambulance for a haemodynamically-stable patient with a negative necrotising/shock screen, when urgent same-day in-person assessment by own transport is appropriate; unnecessary ambulance use and emergency-system load, but far less harmful than under-triage.
